coredata.dc:description: Point-of-care (POC) sensors possess significant potential for various applications such as environmental monitoring and clinical diagnosis. However, POC sensors often encounter challenges regarding accuracy and sensitivity. Herein, we present a facile, cost-efficient and high-yield synthesis strategy of strongly blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) from Prunus mume Carbonisatus (PMC) for fabricating surface patterned fluorescent hydrogel POC sensors with ultra-high sensitivity and accuracy. By rationally integrating Fe3+-responsive PMC-CDs with surface patterning enhanced visualization (SPEV) strategy, we achieve accurate POC detection of Fe3+ ions with a visual detection limit of 0.1 μM using the pseudo-color-assisted patterned POC sensors. Benefiting from more diverse and well-controlled visual information provided by the pseudo-color images of patterned hydrogels, the ultrasensitive and accurate visual POC detection of Fe3+ in iron supplements and human serum samples could be easily achieved without using any expensive instruments. This single emitting fluorophore-based surface patterned hydrogel sensing platform exhibits exceptional visual quantitative capability and remarkable convenience, thus holding great promise for visual POC testing.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Point-of-care (POC) sensors possess significant potential for various applications such as environmental monitoring and clinical diagnosis. However, POC sensors often encounter challenges regarding accuracy and sensitivity. Herein, we present a facile, cost-efficient and high-yield synthesis strategy of strongly blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) from Prunus mume Carbonisatus (PMC) for fabricating surface patterned fluorescent hydrogel POC sensors with ultra-high sensitivity and accuracy. By rationally integrating Fe3+-responsive PMC-CDs with surface patterning enhanced visualization (SPEV) strategy, we achieve accurate POC detection of Fe3+ ions with a visual detection limit of 0.1 μM using the pseudo-color-assisted patterned POC sensors. Benefiting from more diverse and well-controlled visual information provided by the pseudo-color images of patterned hydrogels, the ultrasensitive and accurate visual POC detection of Fe3+ in iron supplements and human serum samples could be easily achieved without using any expensive instruments. This single emitting fluorophore-based surface patterned hydrogel sensing platform exhibits exceptional visual quantitative capability and remarkable convenience, thus holding great promise for visual POC testing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Point-of-care (POC) biochemical sensors are a portable and convenient detection approach with extensive application potential in many fields such as clinical diagnosis [1,2], environmental monitoring [3] and food safety [4]. Unlike other POC detection methods, optical detection does not require complex instruments and enables non-invasive and naked-eye detection, making it a cost-effective and convenient approach [5]. However, optical POC sensors currently face challenges related to detection accuracy and sensitivity. To address these challenges and advance optical biochemical sensors into highly practical POC devices, researchers have developed novel responsive optical nanomaterials and signal transduction techniques in recent decades [6–8]. Despite some progress, the synthesis methods for these nanomaterials are generally expensive and unsustainable, and the sensitivity of existing optical POC sensors falls short of the requirement for accurate naked-eye detection [9–11]. Consequently, there is a significant yet challenging need to develop a highly convenient and sustainable optical POC sensor with ultra-high sensitivity.

Among various optical signal transduction techniques, fluorescence-based sensing systems have gained tremendous attention in POC testing due to their low cost, high sensitivity, rapid response, and the wide range of fluorophores available [12–14]. A multitude of fluorescence-based POC sensors for naked-eye detection have been developed by integrating various responsive fluorophores into different sensing substrates [15–17]. However, these current fluorescence signal transduction techniques still encounter challenges in achieving a balance between sensitivity and convenience. Consequently, they only permit a rough qualitative (yes/no) or semi-quantitative determination of the target analytes in POC testing [18–20]. In our previous work, we developed a novel surface patterning enhanced visualization (SPEV) strategy utilizing hierarchically porous metal-organic frameworks (HP-MOFs) as a ratiometric fluorescent (RF) probe for highly sensitive and accurate POC detection [21], benefiting from the excellent mass transfer performance of HP-MOFs and significantly enriched optical information emitted from surface patterned sensing platforms. However, the synthesis processes for HP-MOFs-based RF composites are complex and time-consuming. Additionally, the more interesting and convenient possibility of enhancing detection sensitivity in optical POC sensors through a single emitting fluorophore-based SPEV strategy has not been explored, which would significantly simplify the design of POC sensing systems.

Compared to other types of fluorescence probes, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant interest as fluorescence probes in POC testing due to their easy preparation process, excellent photostability, and wide functional diversity [22–24]. Currently, CDs are generally prepared from chemically-synthesized materials or natural materials. Biomass, as an important class of natural materials, has gained attention as an appealing precursor for environmentally friendly synthesis of fluorescent CDs due to its abundant and renewable sources, cost-effectiveness and ease of large-scale production [25–27]. Hydrothermal and pyrolytic processes have been commonly used to synthesize biomass-derived CDs due to their practicality and simplicity [28–30]. However, these methods commonly require high temperatures and prolonged reaction times, leading to high energy consumption, limited product yield and quantum yield (QY). Therefore, alternative pathways need to be explored for energy-efficient and high-yield synthesis of highly fluorescent biomass-derived CDs.

Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM), derived from natural materials through specific processing procedures, has been studied for its potential in producing value-added fluorescent CDs used in drug therapy, sensing, and bioimaging [31,32]. However, the production of TCM-derived CDs in these studies heavily relies on traditional pyrolysis or hydrothermal methods with high energy-consuming and low yield. In a recent study, inspired by the similarities between the carbonization process of TCM and the pyrolytic synthesis process of biomass-derived CDs, our group proposed a new method for preparing TCM-based CDs with high QY through room temperature solvent extraction of processed TCM (i.e., rice fried Codonopsis pilosula) [33]. This approach eliminates the need for prolonged heating, thus addressing the issues such as high energy consumption and expensive heating devices. Moreover, TCM have diverse sources and a well-established industry chain in China, facilitating large-scale production of CDs. However, this room temperature solvent extraction method still faces challenges, including low CD yield (< 1%) and time-consuming synthesis processes (> 4 h), thereby limiting its practical potential. Hence, developing a facile, cost-efficient and high-yield synthesis strategy for processed TCM-derived CDs is of great practical importance for ultrasensitive visual POC testing.

Meanwhile, as one of the most important environmentally and physiologically relevant metal ions, Fe3+ performs crucial and versatile roles in environmental protection and disease diagnosis. Compare with traditional complex-instrument based Fe3+ detection strategies, fluorescence-based Fe3+ visualization techniques have aroused increasing attention for its high availability, diverse choices of fluorescence probes and multi-color emission. Various types of responsive fluorophores have been developed to visually detect Fe3+, including carbon dots [34], metal nanoclusters/nanoparticles [35], fluorescent metal organic frame-based probes [36], semiconductor quantum dots [37] and organic dyes [38]. However, the synthesis of these Fe3+-responsive probes often involves complex procedures and high costs. Additionally, most fluorescence-based visualization methods in previous studies lack the capability for precise quantitative POC detection of Fe3+. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop cost-effective, highly precise and sensitive fluorescence-based POC sensors for visualizing Fe3+.

Herein, we present a facile, cost-efficient and high-yield synthesis strategy of processed TCM (Prunus mume Carbonisatus, PMC)-derived CDs to fabricate surface patterned fluorescent hydrogel sensors for SPEV-based quantitative POC detection of trace Fe3+ in complex samples ( Scheme 1). By integrating Fe3+-responsive PMC-CDs with SPEV technique, we achieve accurate POC detection of Fe3+ ions with a significant 102-fold increase in sensitivity. Our synthesis method involves a simple and efficient approach, i.e., a NaOH/H2O2 post-degradation treatment, resulting in PMC-CDs with a high quantum yield (QY) of 9.8% and a product yield of 22.4%. These optimized blue-emissive PMC-CDs are then incorporated into tailor-made surface patterned hydrogel carriers to fabricate the ‘Piggy’ and some other patterned POC hydrogel sensors. The ‘Piggy’ sensor exhibits abundant and easy-to-read pattern information, which is easier to be distinguished by human eyes even at low target concentration after customized pseudo-color postprocessing. Importantly, the detection limit (0.1 μM) of the developed ‘Piggy’ POC sensor for naked-eye detecting Fe3+ was nearly equivalent to that of spectrophotometer-based techniques, which has been successfully used for ultrasensitive and quantitative POC detection of Fe3+ in complex sample matrices without expensive instruments, including iron supplements and human serum samples. Additionally, the SPEV-derived pattern block-based RGB (PB-RGB) assay was found to exhibit enhanced applicability for quantitative detection of trace Fe3+ compared to the traditional RGB assay. Therefore, single emitting CDs-based and pseudo-color-assisted SPEV strategy exhibits great potential for fabricating next generation optical POC sensors with ultra-high sensitivity and excellent practicability.

Prunus mume Carbonisatus (PMC) were purchased from Tianzhi traditional Chinese medicine Co., Ltd (Luzhou, Sichuan, China), which met the quality criteria of Chinese Pharmacopoeia. Quinine sulfate dihydrate, disodium edetate dihydrate (EDTA) and agarose were bought from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. Ferric chloride, ferrous chloride, copper sulfate, silver nitrate and other metal ions were all purchased from Huaxia Reagent Co., Ltd. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), trichloroacetic acid (TCA, 20%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 97%), hydrochloric acid (36–38%) and the other chemical reagents were all obtained from Sichuan Kelong Reagent Co., Ltd. Iron supplements (trade name: Nature Made Iron) was purchased from local drugstore (Luzhou, Sichuan, China). Dialysis membranes were supplied by Yuanye Biotech Co., Ltd. The ultrapure water was prepared by AWL-1002-H ultrapure water system. The human serum sample was provided by a healthy volunteer from our laboratory in Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, China.

The UV–visible absorption spectra of obtained CDs were analyzed using a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence properties of PMC-CDs solutions were examined using a LS55 luminescence spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured using an IRAffinity-1S spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The elemental component analysis of the CDs was conducted using an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Electron, USA). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were observed with an electron microscope (JEM-1200EX, accelerating voltage, 120 kV). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed using a Shimadzu X-6100 X-Ray diffractometer. The Fe3+ content in human serum samples and iron supplements was determined using the Agilent 7800 inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) system.

The synthesis of PMC-CDs involved the rapid breakdown of insufficiently carbonized lignin and carbohydrate within PMC under heating conditions by using diluted NaOH/H2O2 solution as an oxidant [29,39]. Initially, PMC (0.2 g) was pulverized, and the resulting brown-black powder was evenly dispersed in a round-bottom flask (100 mL) containing NaOH solution of desired concentration. Subsequently, a specific amount of H2O2 was gradually added to the PMC dispersion solution with vigorous stirring. The mixture was then subjected to boiling extraction at 100 °C for 30 min, resulting in the formation of a golden-brown transparent solution. The insoluble precipitate was filtered out, and the obtained filtrate was adjusted to a nearly neutral pH using HCl. It was then dialyzed against ultrapure water using a dialysis membrane (MWCO 1000 Da) for 24 h. The resulting dialyzed solution was subsequently filtered using a 0.22 µm membrane filter and freeze-dried. The obtained powder of PMC-CDs was dissolved in ultrapure water to achieve a concentration of 20 mg/mL and stored in a refrigerator (4 °C) for further application.

The detection of Fe3+ was performed in a solution of sodium acetate buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.0). Briefly, a quartz cuvette was filled with 20 μL of PMC-CDs solution and mixed with 100 μL of Fe3+ standard stock solutions of varying concentrations, along with 100 μL of 2.5 mM EDTA solution. Then, the solutions were supplemented with sodium acetate buffer, resulting in a final volume of 4 mL. After thorough mixing and incubation for 5 min, the fluorescence emission spectra and fluorescence intensity at 430 nm were recorded before and after the addition of Fe3+, respectively, under an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. In order to assess the specific sensing ability of the prepared PMC-CDs for Fe3+, other metal ions with a final concentration of 200 μM were also detected using a similar procedure. All experiments were conducted at room temperature and repeated in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The visual POC detection of Fe3+ based on the fluorescent hydrogel sensor
ce:section.ce:para: A novel hydrogel-based sensing platform was developed for ultrasensitive and quantitative POC detection of Fe3+, employing our previously established complex instrument-free and cost-effective SPEV strategy [21]. The hydrogel platform was prepared by adding agarose (0.3 g) to 17 mL of sodium acetate buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.0), and the mixture was completely dissolved by boiling for 5 min. Next, 3 mL of the as-prepared PMC-CDs solution was added and uniformly dispersed into the agarose solution. The resulting solution was then poured into circular molds without surface patterns and cooled to room temperature, forming PMC-CDs/agarose hybrid hydrogel sheets. These circular hydrogel sheets were subsequently immersed in Fe3+ solutions of varying concentrations for 40 min. Fluorescence images of the hydrogel sheets were captured using a digital camera (Sony DSC-RX100) under a 360 nm UV light emitted from a portable ultraviolet lamp, enabling naked-eye detection. Additionally, 3D-printed silicone molds with various patterns (lollipop, paw, Christmas tree, flower, snowflake, fruit, bear, dinosaur, and piggy) were employed to create surface-patterned PMC-CDs-based hybrid hydrogels. The subsequent visual POC detection procedures remained similar to those of the unpatterned hydrogel sheets.

Fluorescence images were subjected to image analysis on a Hewlett-Packard computer running Microsoft Windows 10. Customized colormaps were developed using MATLAB codes written by our group, and the associated pseudo-color processing was carried out with MATLAB software. To assess the practicality of the proposed pseudo-color-assisted SPEV strategy for quantitative POC detection of Fe3+, real-world samples, including human serum samples and iron supplements, were employed. Human serum samples were obtained from a healthy adult volunteer and treated with TCA to release Fe3+ ions from proteins. Specifically, an equivalent volume of fresh blood and 20% (w/v) TCA solution were thoroughly mixed and subjected to heating at 100 °C for 10 min. Centrifugation at 5000 rpm was then employed to collect the resulting supernatant. Given that iron was present in the serum as ferrous ions, H2O2 solution was added to the supernatant to oxidize Fe2+ ions to Fe3+ ions for subsequent analysis. Similarly, tablet powder from iron supplements was treated with ample H2O2 to convert Fe2+ completely into Fe3+. Centrifugation and filtration steps were implemented to remove insoluble excipients for further detection. The quantification of Fe3+ content in both human serum and iron supplement samples was achieved using the pseudo-color-assisted SPEV-based POC testing platform, ICP-MS and fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively.

As demonstrated in our previous study [33], some common and cost-effective processed products of TCM naturally contain readily available fluorescent CDs and may serve as more suitable precursors for cost-efficient and sustainable synthesis of biomass-derived CDs without the necessity of sophisticated equipment. The key challenge to address is the development of a high-yield and time-efficient method for extracting TCM-derived CDs. In this study, alkaline peroxide treatment, a commonly employed technique for delignification and pulp bleaching [40], was hypothesized to facilitate the extraction of fluorescent CDs from processed TCM. To achieve this, several commonly used processed TCM such as Pollen Typhae Carbonisatus, Crinis Carbonisatus, and Phellodendri Cortex Carbonisatus have been considered as potential carbon sources for synthesizing fluorescent CDs. All the extracts of TCMs displayed varying degrees of fluorescence after undergoing similar degradation and dialysis treatments, yielding considerable amounts of fluorescent products. Among them, the fluorescent products extracted from Prunus mume Carbonisatus (PMC) exhibited the optimal fluorescence performance. Therefore, we selected PMC as a representative renewable biochar source for alkaline peroxide-assisted solvent extraction of potential CDs. As depicted in Fig. 1a and b, the increase in the amounts of H2O2 and NaOH corresponded to a significant reduction in the insoluble residue in the post-reaction solution, accompanied by a gradual lightening of the solution color from dark black to light yellow. Concomitantly, the yield of purified PMC-CDs and their quantum yield (QY) exhibited continuous increments (Fig. 1c and d). Notably, when the extraction solvent contained 20 wt% H2O2 and 0.5 M NaOH, a high yield of 21.9 wt% PMC-CDs with a maximum QY of 9.1% was achieved. For comparison, the product yield, QY, and synthesis time of PMC-CDs obtained through peroxide-assisted solvent extraction without NaOH, ultrasonic-assisted solvent extraction, as well as previously reported biomass-derived CDs synthesized via hydrothermal treatment, are listed in Table S1. Evidently, the synthesis approach of CDs through alkaline peroxide-assisted extraction from processed TCM exhibits higher preparation efficiency than other biomass-derived CD synthesis methods, due to its higher yield and significantly shorter synthesis time (30 min). The significant improvement in preparation efficiency can be attributed to the rapid dissociation of H2O2 into HOO- and oxygen free radicals under alkaline and elevated temperature conditions, leading to the oxidation and degradation of organic amorphous carbon within PMC, such as incompletely carbonized fragments of lignin and carbohydrates [29]. As a result, stable crystalline carbon was retained, thus facilitating the formation of PMC-CDs.

The FTIR spectra of PMC-CDs (Fig. S1a and b) exhibit a sharp increase in the intensity of -OH groups and a gradual decrease in the intensity of CO groups as the concentrations of NaOH and H2O2 increase, indicating the substantial destruction of carbonyl groups and the formation of a greater number of hydroxyl groups in PMC-CDs. The XPS survey spectra reveal that the prepared CDs predominantly consist of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen elements with varying proportions (Fig. S1c-f). As the concentrations of NaOH and H2O2 rise, the O/C atomic ratio shows a significant increase from 0.34 to 0.41, indicating enhanced oxidation of PMC biochar. The high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1 s in PMC-CDs (Fig. S2a-d) show a notable increase in the percentage of -OH groups and a considerable decrease in the content of CO bonds with higher concentrations of NaOH and H2O2. This further confirms the reduction of conjugated double bonds and the presence of more hydroxyl groups on the surface of PMC-CDs. Consequently, a substantial proportion of electron-accepting carbonyl groups, which can function as fluorescence quenchers, are transformed into electron-donating hydroxyl groups, leading to a significant increase in the QY of PMC-CDs [41].

The morphology of CDs was examined using TEM to investigate their structure under optimal preparation conditions. The prepared CDs are observed to be monodisperse spherical particles with an average diameter of approximately 2.5 nm (Fig. 1e and f). High-resolution TEM reveals well-defined lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystallographic facet of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1e inset). Furthermore, the XRD pattern of PMC-CDs demonstrates a distinct diffraction peak at 2θ = 22° and a weak peak at 2θ = 41°, corresponding to the lattice spacing of (002) and (100) graphite carbon [24], respectively (Fig. S3). The absorption and fluorescence properties of PMC-CDs were analyzed using UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. The UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 1g) reveals a broad absorption band that extends up to 500 nm without noticeable features, while the PMC-CDs aqueous solution exhibits strong blue fluorescence under UV irradiation at 365 nm (inset in Fig. 1g). The maximum emission is observed at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm, which can be attributed to the n-π * transition of heteroatomic functional groups present on the surface of the CDs [42]. Similar to most fluorescent CDs, PMC-CDs exhibit excitation-dependent emission behavior, with a peak emission at 430 nm (Fig. S4A), which can be attributed to heterogeneity in surface states and differences in CD size [43]. Additionally, as demonstrated in Fig. S5, PMC-CDs exhibit remarkable photostability across different environmental conditions, suggesting their potential for practical sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of PMC-CDs to Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by previous studies on the quenching properties of metal ions on biomass-derived CDs, we hypothesized that PMC-CDs may also exhibit a sensitive fluorescence response to metal ions. To validate the hypothesis, PMC-CDs solutions were mixed with different metal ions, and the resulting fluorescence intensities were compared (Fig. S6). Several metal ions, including Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, and Ba2+, demonstrated varying degrees of quenching effect on the fluorescence of PMC-CDs. Fortunately, the interference from these metal ions could be effectively eliminated by the masking effect of EDTA, enabling a highly selective fluorescence response towards Fe3+. Additionally, the quenching effect of Fe3+ remained largely unaffected in the presence of other interfering metal ions, further confirming the excellent selectivity of PMC-CDs for Fe3+ detection. The prepared CDs also exhibited an extremely sensitive response to Fe3+ (Fig. S4b), with a linear relationship observed between the fluorescent quenching ratio ((F0-F)/F0) and Fe3+ concentration within the range of 0.5–60 μM (Fig. 1h inset). The detection limit of Fe3+ was calculated to be approximately 30.0 nM using the equation of 3σ/S, which is significantly lower than the permissible concentration of Fe3+ in drinking water (5.4 μM) and comparable to or even better than the Fe3+ sensing performance of previously reported CDs-based fluorescent probes (Table S2). Thus, PMC-CDs hold great potential as highly sensitive nanoprobe for Fe3+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanism of Fe3+ to PMC-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The mean fluorescence lifetime of PMC-CDs was determined to be 3.57 ns (Fig. S7a). Upon mixing with Fe3+, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs decreased significantly to 2.81 ns, ruling out the possibility of static quenching or inner filter effects. The Stern-Volmer quenching constant KSV (3.01 × 104 M−1) obtained at 70 ℃ (Fig. S7b) was considerably higher than that at 30 ℃ (2.0 × 104 M−1), indicating a dynamic quenching characteristic [44]. Furthermore, the absorption spectrum of the PMC-CDs/Fe3+ mixture displayed a high degree of overlap with the combined absorption spectra of PMC-CDs and Fe3+ (Fig. S7c), further indicating that there is no formation of ground state complex and that the observed quenching of CDs is attributable to dynamic quenching. Specifically, when metal ions are mixed with PMC-CDs, Fe3+ selectively coordinates with the functional groups on the CD surfaces due to its stronger binding preference. This leads to the non-radiative transfer of excited electrons from CDs to the vacant 3d orbitals of Fe3+, resulting in fluorescence quenching [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Unpatterned hydrogel-based visual POC detection of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Utilizing the strong fluorescence response of PMC-CDs to Fe3+, we developed a conventional hydrogel-based visual POC sensor by incorporating PMC-CDs into agarose, a commonly available non-luminous substrate. Optimal naked-eye discrimination of Fe3+ concentrations could be achieved when 3 mg/mL PMC-CDs existed in the hydrogel substrate (Fig. S8a) under the illumination of a UV lamp (365 nm). On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity of PMC-CDs hydrogel coexisted with Fe3+ gradually decreased until the incubating time exceeded 40 min (Fig. S8b). By contrast, the quenching of PMC-CDs solution in the presence of 70 μM Fe3+ could be rapidly completed within 2 min (Fig. S9). The significant decreased quenching rate revealed that the mass transfer of Fe3+ onto the surface of PMC-CDs could be effectively controlled within the hydrogel medium and was much slower than that occurred in solution, which were consistent with our previous finding [21]. Furthermore, the excellent selectivity of the hydrogel-based visual POC sensor was verified due to the exclusive and remarkable quenching effects induced by Fe3+ (Fig. S10). Additionally, as the Fe3+ content gradually increased from 0 to 90 μM, the color brightness of the unpatterned hydrogels exhibited a decreasing trend ( Fig. 2ai). However, the differences in brightness between adjacent hydrogels were imperceptible to the naked eye unless the Fe3+ concentration interval exceeded 30 μM. In other words, the confidence interval required for identifying Fe3+ concentration was higher than 30 μM. Therefore, the prepared unpatterned PMC-CDs hydrogel-based sensor is limited to qualitative Fe3+ detection and is insufficient for the sensitive and accurate visual POC detection of trace amounts of Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface patterning enhanced visual POC detection of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The evaluation of hydrogel-based visual POC sensors often relies on analyzing color changes in terms of brightness or hue. However, the traditional visualization methods can only provide limited color information, making it challenging for the human eye to discern due to the subtle and minimal changes in brightness or hue [46]. Inspired by the multi-perceptual characteristic of human visual system, our group has recently successfully developed a SPEV technology for precise visual quantitative detection of trace analytes in complex samples [21]. Similarly, we hypothesized that incorporating spatial patterns into PMC-CDs hydrogel could generate richer and more diverse optical information, thereby improving the performance of fluorescence-based POC sensors. To test this hypothesis, a series of surface patterned PMC-CDs hydrogels were soaked in Fe3+ solutions with concentrations ranging from 0 to 90 μM. As shown in Figure 2aii-x, the complexity of the patterns led to increasingly noticeable visual differences between adjacent hydrogels. In particular, the visual differences between each adjacent ‘Piggy’ (the most sophisticated pattern) could be visually distinguished based on the patterned color information. Accordingly, the confidence interval for identifying Fe3+ concentration decreased to approximately 10 μM. These findings affirm the efficacy of the developed SPEV strategy in enhancing the visual POC detection performance of the hydrogel sensor.

Next, the influences of surface pattern parameters on the detection performance of the visual point-of-care (POC) sensor were investigated. In this study, a range of custom-designed ‘Piggy’ molds with varying depth parameters were employed to fabricate ‘Piggy’ patterned hydrogel sensors for the visual detection of Fe3+ concentration (ranging from 0 to 90 μM), as demonstrated in Fig. S11. The resulting ‘Piggy’ hydrogels displayed uniform heights in the head (7 mm), ears (2 mm), and body (4 mm), while the heights of the eyes, cheek, mouth, and hands sections varied from 1 to 5 mm. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, an increase in the heights of the protruding sections resulted in more three-dimensional and hierarchical visual effects, along with stronger localized brightness contrast. By leveraging the richer and more diverse visual information, particularly from the fluorescence images of the six protruding characteristic sections of the ‘Piggy’, the visual distinctions between each adjacent ‘Piggy’ became more detailed and readily distinguishable to the naked eye. The optimal resolution was achieved when the height of the specific sections increased to 5 mm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pseudo-color-assisted patterning enhanced visual POC detection of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Although the surface patterning strategy has effectively improved the Fe3+ visual POC detection performance of hydrogel sensors, the minor visual differences of the monochromatic patterns were difficult to be distinguished by human eyes when the coexisted Fe3+ concentration changed at an interval of 1 μM ( Fig. 4a). To address this issue, we employed the pseudo-color-assisted SPEV strategy to further enhance the visual POC detection performance. The details of the pseudo-color processing procedure can be found in our previous study [21]. In Fig. S12e, it can be observed that the hue of the colored images gradually changes from yellowish green to dull blue as the Fe3+ concentration increases from 0 to 90 μM, corresponding to a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity. As opposed to the monochromatic unpatterned fluorescence images, which are challenging to accurately distinguish (Figure 2ai), the colored images provide additional hue information that improves the distinguishability between each adjacent hydrogel when the coexisting Fe3+ content increases from 0 to 40 μM (Fig. S12e). Additionally, the pseudo-color-assisted strategy is employed for mapping different surface-patterned fluorescence images (Figure 2aii-x and Fig. 3a). As depicted in Figure 2bii-x and Fig. 3b, with an increase in pattern complexity and the height of the protruding blocks in the ‘Piggy’ morphology, the protruding characteristic blocks in the colored images offer more abundant and multifaceted visual information (including color brightness, tonality, contrast, shape, texture, and area) to reflect subtle optical signal changes caused by variations in Fe3+ concentration. Consequently, these changes become easier for human eyes to discern.

Motivated by the exceptional visual resolution, we systematically investigated the potential application of pseudo-color-assisted surface patterned PMC-CDs hydrogel sensors for ultrasensitive visual POC quantitative detection of Fe3+. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, based on the regular color and pattern variations of the colored images of ‘Piggy’ hydrogels, especially on the protruding characteristic blocks, the visual differences between each adjacent ‘Piggy’ were easily discernible as the coexisting Fe3+ concentration increased from 1 to 5 μM. However, discrimination became challenging when the Fe3+ concentration changed from 6 to 7 μM. Actually, the gray values mapped by ‘jet’ ranged from 0 to 255 were wider than the available gray value ranges (0−208) of original grayscale images, which means that the extensive hue of rainbow colors provided by ‘jet’ colormap was not fully utilized to capture subtle differences between each adjacent ‘Piggy’. To address this limitation, we developed a MATLAB algorithm to flexibly customize gray value ranges for rainbow color mapping. Utilizing this algorithm, pseudo-color processing was performed with customized colormaps mapping the gray values within the ranges of 0–208, 0–180, and 0–160 (Fig. 4c-e). Notably, Fig. 4d exhibited the most distinct color and texture difference between each set of protruding characteristic blocks. Consequently, an upper limit of 180 was selected for this mapping. Furthermore, the colored images obtained with the customized ranges of 10–180, 30–180, 50–180 and 70–180 are displayed in Fig. 4f-i. Increasing the lower limit from 0 to 50 gradually normalized the redundant background color information, maximizing the utilization of the wide rainbow color tonality. However, further increasing the lower limit would result in the excessive loss of useful color and pattern information. Therefore, the optimal gray value ranges for color mapping were determined to be 50–180 and the resulting colored images (Fig. 4h) exhibited excellent distinguishability, allowing for visual discrimination of Fe3+ contents ranging from 1 to 7 μM at a concentration interval of 1 μM. Additionally, as illustrated in Fig. S13a and b, after incubating six identical ‘Piggy’ hydrogels with different Fe3+ standard solutions (5.0, 10.0, and 15.0 μM), the visual information depicted by the protruding characteristic blocks in each series of fluorescence images and customized colored ‘Piggy’ images remained nearly identical. This result demonstrates the remarkable robustness and good repeatability of the pseudo-color-assisted SPEV method for visual POC quantitative detection of Fe3+.

Based on the customized pseudo-color mapping strategy, we investigated the feasibility of sensitive and accurate POC detection of Fe3+ at lower concentrations. As depicted in Fig. 5a, the subtle visual differences in the monochromatic luminescent ‘Piggy’ were imperceptible to human eyes when the coexisting Fe3+ concentration changed at intervals of 0.1 μM. However, after applying the customized pseudo-color processing, the colored images displayed regular changes in color and texture between each protruding characteristic block, allowing easy distinction as the coexisting Fe3+ concentration increased from 0.1 to 0.8 μM (Fig. 5b). Moreover, the variations in block outline and decrease in area in the 'body' block served as auxiliary criteria for distinguishing the corresponding Fe3+ content. These findings indicate that the pseudo-color-assisted SPEV method has a resolution of approximately 0.1 μM for distinguishing Fe3+ concentration intervals, which is comparable to that of spectrophotometer-based measurements (30.0 nM). For comparison, we also performed the same customized pseudo-color processing for fluorescence images of unpatterned hydrogels with different Fe3+ concentrations at intervals of 1 and 0.1 μM (Fig. S14a and c). As a result, the patternless colored images were difficult to discriminate when the Fe3+ concentration ranged from 0 to 7 μM at 1 μM intervals (Fig. S14b) or from 0.1 to 0.8 μM at 0.1 μM intervals (Fig. S14d). A comparison of the POC detecting performance of the pseudo-color-assisted surface patterned PMC-CDs hydrogel sensor with existing Fe3+-selective visual sensors and the traditional unpatterned PMC-CDs fluorescent hydrogel sensor is presented in Table S3. Our pseudo-color SPEV technique provides more abundant, well-controlled, and multifaceted visual information to the human visual system, enabling a significant shift from qualitative/semi-quantitative to quantitative visual POC detection with ultrahigh sensitivity and excellent accuracy.

Additionally, the other interesting possibility for accurately detecting Fe3+ with higher concentration range (100–1000 μM) was also explored (Fig. 5c). It should be noted that the traditional unpatterned visual sensors typically struggle to visually distinguish the nearly complete quenching effects at high quencher concentrations. Surprisingly, the customized pseudo-color processing allowed for the clear visualization of regular color, outline, and area changes in the colored images of ‘Piggy’ hydrogels coexisting with 100–1000 μM of Fe3+, facilitating easy differentiation by human eyes at a concentration interval of 100 μM (Fig. 5d). This indicates that the developed pseudo-color-assisted SPEV strategy significantly enhances the visual detection performance for Fe3+ at ultrahigh concentrations, thereby expanding the application scope and practicality of visual fluorescent sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Accurate visual POC detection of Fe3+ in iron supplements and human serum
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality of the PMC-CDs-based ‘Piggy’ POC sensor was assessed through visually quantitative determination of Fe3+ in real-world samples. In Fig. 6a, the ‘Piggy’ images treated with various Fe3+ standard solutions (0–90 μM) formed a series of “colorimetric images” for matching Fe3+ concentration. After immersing the optimized ‘Piggy’ hydrogel in a diluted solution of iron supplements, the colored image fell between the scale of 20–30 μM Fe3+ standard solutions (Fig. 6b and c). Subsequent detailed image matching, using color and texture information from each protruding block of ‘Piggy’, revealed that the visual characteristics of the tested colored image closely resembled that of the 23.2 μM standard solution, which showed high consistency with fluorescence spectrophotometer (23.6 μM) and ICP-MS (22.3 μM) results. Employing a similar image matching strategy, the visually quantitative determination of Fe3+ in a diluted human serum sample yielded a result of approximately 4.1 μM (Fig. S15a-c), demonstrating good agreement with spectrophotometer (3.7 μM) and ICP-MS (3.9 μM) analyses. Overall, the pseudo-color-assisted SPEV-based POC testing platform exhibited ultralow detection limit and excellent accuracy for determining Fe3+ levels in complex sample matrices with the naked eye, comparable to sophisticated instrument-based detection techniques. Moreover, as indicated in Table S4, the overall instrument cost of our Fe3+ detection method was significantly lower than sophisticated instrument-based and other visual detection techniques. Considering the excellent sensitivity and practicality of the developed single emitting CDs-based surface patterned hydrogel sensing platform, it holds great potential for various POC applications.

In addition to being directly perceptible by the human eye through the pseudo-color-assisted SPEV strategy, the abundant optical information provided by the PMC-CDs-based ‘Piggy’ hydrogel may also be effectively analyzed using image processing software. To this end, we further conducted the traditional RGB assay and the SPEV-derived PB-RGB assay. For the traditional assay, we measured the average RGB values at five random points in unpatterned fluorescence images. The latter was based on the RGB-weighted averages of the “hands” blocks of the ‘Piggy’ fluorescence images extracted by ImageJ software. As depicted in Fig. S16b, a highly linear response was obtained between the B values of the “hands” blocks and the Fe3+ concentration (0–80 μM), with a detection limit of 0.83 μM. The PB-RGB quantitative results for Fe3+ in iron supplements (21.0 μM) and human serum (4.7 μM) were basically consistent with the results obtained from fluorospectrophotometer and ICP-MS analyses. In contrast, the traditional RGB assay (Fig. S16a) showed relatively poor linearity and large quantitative error due to the non-uniform quenching effect observed in the unpatterned pseudo-color images. Therefore, the novel SPEV-derived PB-RGB assay provides another reliable and convenient method for quantitative detection without requiring complicated instruments.

In summary, we successfully synthesized strongly blue-emitting PMC-CDs and rationally designed surface patterned hydrogel sensors for ultrasensitive and quantitative visual POC detection. By integrating the high QY of single emitting PMC-CDs and the abundant visual information provided by the patterned design, the visual sensitivity of the low-cost, easy-to-prepare and complex instrument-free hydrogel-based POC sensors increased over a hundred times. Consequently, the promising potential of a single emitting stimuli-responsive fluorophore for accurate visual POC detection was achieved. Another interesting SPEV-derived PB-RGB assay for software-based quantitative detection of trace Fe3+ was also demonstrated. Moreover, this approach holds great potential for developing other types of patterned optical POC sensors for specific analytes by integrating stimuli-responsive optical nanomaterials and SPEV strategy. This study not only establishes an efficient and high-yield method for preparing fluorescent CDs derived from TCM but also demonstrates the immense potential of SPEV technique in visual POC diagnosis and other monitoring applications.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yan Qiu is a Pharmacist in Drug Clinical Trial Institution, The Affiliated Hospital of Southwest Medical University, China. Her major research interests include the green synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials and the preparation of non-toxic hair dye.

Lan Xia is a Ph.D student in School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, China. Her major research interests include the green synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials and preparation of novel functional surface coating.

Rui Shi is an associate chief physician in The Affiliated Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine of Southwest Medical University, China. Her major research interests include the synthesis and application of luminescent functionalized nanomaterials in clinical diagnosis.
Long Yuan is an MSc student in School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, China. His major research interests include the synthesis and application of fluorescent nanomaterials.

Yuanhua Zhang is a Pharmacist in Drug Clinical Trial Institution, The Affiliated Hospital of Southwest Medical University, China. His major research interests include the synthesis and application of fluorescent nanomaterials and functional metal-organic frameworks.

Anqi Chen is a Ph.D student in State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, China. Her major research interests include the synthesis and application of fluorescent organic nanoparticles.

Kai Zhou is a senior engineer in Analytical and Testing Center, Chongqing University, China. His major research interests include the development of luminescent functionalized nanomaterials and the application of biological imaging.

Hailong Wu is a professor in State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, China. His major research interests include Chemistry, Biochemistry, Genetics and Molecular Biology, Computer Science, Chemical Engineering and so on.

Kailian Zhang is a professor in School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, China. She received her bachelor's degree in Pharmaceutical Chemistry in 1988 from China Pharmaceutical University and master’s degree in Chinese and Western Integrative Medicine in 2006 from Southwest Medical University. Her major research interests include pharmaceutical analysis and drug metabolism.

Zhining Xia is a professor in School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, China. He received his PhD degree in analytical chemistry from University of Copenhagen in 1995. During 1995–1996, he was a postdoctoral research fellow in Faculty of Science, the University of Copenhagen. His research is focused on the development of chromatography and spectrometry analysis technologies.

Qifeng Fu is a professor in School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, China. He received his bachelor's degree in Applied Chemistry in 2012 and PhD degree in Chemical Engineering and Technology in 2017 from Chongqing University. His research is focused on the synthesis and application of functional fluorescent nanomaterials, functional surface coating materials and the analytical methodologies on capillary electrophoresis.
